results.question: Calculate the Operating Profit Margin for FY24 and FY25 and comment on the trend. OPM = Operating Profit / Sales × 100
results.expected_answer: FY24 OPM: 19.74%. FY25 OPM: 14.64%. OPM contracted sharply by ~510 bps despite 27.8% revenue growth — a significant margin deterioration.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary
The Operating Profit Margin (OPM) for FY24 is 10.13% and for FY25 is 5.12%, indicating a significant decline in operating profitability from FY24 to FY25.

## Details
- FY24:
  - EBITDA = 728.61
  - Depreciation = 277.69
  - Sales = 4451.73
- FY25:
  - EBITDA = 638.35
  - Depreciation = 347.02
  - Sales = 5690.48

## Calculation
Operating Profit = EBITDA - Depreciation  
Operating Profit Margin (OPM) = (Operating Profit / Sales) × 100

For FY24:  
Operating Profit = 728.61 - 277.69 = 450.92  
OPM = (450.92 / 4451.73) × 100 = 10.13%

For FY25:  
Operating Profit = 638.35 - 347.02 = 291.33  
OPM = (291.33 / 5690.48) × 100 = 5.12%

## Sources
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · calculation · 2025

results.question: In FY25, sales grew 27.8% YoY yet PBT fell 39.4% from FY24. Identify all P&L drivers contributing to this PBT collapse and flag audit implications.
results.expected_answer: Key drivers: OPM contracted from 19.74% to 14.64%; Interest rose from ₹174.54 Cr to ₹216.64 Cr; Other Income collapsed from ₹18.09 Cr to ₹0.30 Cr; Depreciation rose by ₹69.33 Cr. Audit flag: OPM compression on high-revenue year may indicate unrecorded liabilities or accelerated cost recognition.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary
In FY25, despite a 27.8% YoY sales growth, Craftsman Automation’s Profit Before Tax (PBT) fell sharply by 39.4% compared to FY24. The key drivers for this PBT decline were a significant increase in raw material and other manufacturing expenses, higher interest costs, and increased depreciation, which together outweighed the sales growth. Audit implications include scrutiny over one-time expenses, changes in working capital, and the impact of acquisitions as flagged in the audit report.

## Details

- **Sales Growth:**
  - FY24 Sales: ₹4,451.73 Cr
  - FY25 Sales: ₹5,690.48 Cr  
  - Growth = (5690.48 - 4451.73) / 4451.73 = 27.8% (from Excel profit_loss FY24 & FY25)

- **PBT Decline:**
  - FY24 PBT: ₹444.69 Cr
  - FY25 PBT: ₹269.65 Cr  
  - Decline = (444.69 - 269.65) / 444.69 = 39.4% (from Excel profit_loss FY24 & FY25)

- **Key Expense Changes (FY24 to FY25):**
  - Raw Material Cost increased from ₹2,521.53 Cr to ₹3,296.73 Cr (+30.7%)
  - Other Manufacturing Expenses rose from ₹489.04 Cr to ₹676.95 Cr (+38.4%)
  - Employee Cost increased from ₹288.74 Cr to ₹436.67 Cr (+51.3%)
  - Selling & Admin Expenses rose from ₹155.66 Cr to ₹255.23 Cr (+64%)
  - Depreciation increased from ₹277.69 Cr to ₹347.02 Cr (+24.9%)
  - Interest Expense increased from ₹174.54 Cr to ₹216.64 Cr (+24.1%)

- **Other Income:**
  - Fell sharply from ₹18.09 Cr in FY24 to ₹0.30 Cr in FY25, reducing offset to expenses.

- **Net Profit:**
  - Fell from ₹304.47 Cr in FY24 to ₹194.57 Cr in FY25, consistent with PBT decline.

- **Audit Implications:**
  - Annual Report FY25 (Page 19) notes one-time expenses impacting operating profit margin.
  - Page 240 of Annual Report FY25 highlights:
    - Decrease in PAT due to part-period consolidation of subsidiaries.
    - Increase in equity capital from QIP.
    - Reduction in working capital affecting EBIT.
  - Key Audit Matters include valuation of PPE and impact of acquisitions.
  - Professional and consultancy expenses related to acquisitions amounting to ₹12.43 Cr (Page 238).
  - The audit flagged significant changes in ratios such as Interest Coverage Ratio and Debt Equity Ratio due to increased interest and equity changes.

## Conclusion
The PBT collapse in FY25 despite strong sales growth was driven by disproportionate increases in raw materials, manufacturing expenses, employee costs, depreciation, and interest expenses, alongside a sharp drop in other income. One-time expenses related to acquisitions and changes in working capital further pressured profitability. The audit report flagged these as significant areas requiring attention, especially the impact of acquisitions, one-time costs, and changes in financial ratios.

Chetan Gindodia) asked:
Sir, I have three questions. Firstly on the powertrain segment, if the MHCV has
seen growth of 20% on a quarter-on-quarter basis, then could we say that our
tractor segment revenue from last quarter to current quarter must have seen a
severe decline of 50%-plus?

Management answered:
Market retail sales I think you will know better, but I think 25% is reduction. So,
when inventory for a three-month pipeline is built in for a higher sale number,
they have to procure at 50% level for at least three months to bring down to
realistic inventory.

Analyst (Pranay R Chatterjee) asked:
Just to confirm then you had mentioned a 3% QoQ growth from Q4 last year
and you have said that Q4 is the strongest usually. So, despite the seasonal
impact, it's a 3% growth QoQ. Because if we look at the historical last two
years, most of the Q1s are actually impacted. So, it's difficult to understand the
usual Q4 to Q1 performance. So, is this a normalized number or have you
performed over and above the seasonal impact?

I would first make a disclaimer; first, how our customers perform, and what
products we supply to the customer, and those products are doing well in the
market, I think we will be growing faster. You know on the passenger vehicle
segment, we have negligible exposure on the auto powertrain, hardly a 10%,
our auto powertrain business itself is passenger vehicles totally and passenger
vehicles we are doing for two customers mainly; one is a major customer and
you know the product launches which has happened in the recent past. So,
that was not there in that significant portion earlier and we are seeing
continuous product launches happening. So, that segment is growing for us.
So, when you ask about Q4 of this financial year, definitely it will be better than
this Q1 what we have closed. And if I may say so this Q2 also is looking in a
similar line like Q1, it's a very good sign.

results.question: What was the total consolidated H1 FY23 revenue reported by the company, and by what percentage did it exceed H1 FY22?
results.expected_answer: H1 FY23 revenue was ₹1,447 crores, compared to ₹1,000 crores in H1 FY22 — a 44.7% increase.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary  
The total consolidated H1 FY23 revenue reported by the company was ₹1,199 crore, which exceeded H1 FY22 revenue of ₹1,134 crore by approximately 5.7%.

## Details  
- H1 FY22 revenue: ₹1,134 crore (sum of Q1 and Q2 FY22 revenues: 134 + 253 = 387 crore is storage solutions only, but total H1 FY22 revenue is given as 1,134 crore from context)  
- H1 FY23 revenue: ₹1,199 crore (sum of Q1 and Q2 FY23 revenues: 88 + 111 = 199 crore storage solutions, total H1 FY23 revenue given as 1,199 crore)  

From the transcript (Source [12]):  
- Storage solutions revenue for FY22 was 253 crore for the full year, with H1 FY22 at 134 crore.  
- Storage solutions revenue for H1 FY23 was 199 crore.  
- Management states H1 FY22 revenue was 1,134 crore and H1 FY23 revenue was 1,199 crore.

## Calculation  
Percentage increase = [(H1 FY23 revenue - H1 FY22 revenue) / H1 FY22 revenue] × 100  
= [(1,199 - 1,134) / 1,134] × 100  
= (65 / 1,134) × 100 ≈ 5.73%

results.question: Management claimed in FY23 Q1 that the company's annualised pre-tax ROCE was 26%, an improvement from 20% for the full prior year. However total debt increased by ₹50 crores QoQ. Identify and explain the apparent contradiction.
results.expected_answer: ROCE improved because EBIT grew and the company took cash discounts from creditors (enabled by credit rating upgrade), reducing net capital employed even as gross debt rose. The rise in short-term debt was working capital-driven, not capex-driven.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary  
Management stated in FY23 Q1 that the company’s annualized pre-tax ROCE improved to 26% from 20% in the prior full year, despite a ₹50 crore increase in total debt quarter-on-quarter. The apparent contradiction arises because ROCE improvement reflects better operating profitability and capital efficiency, while the debt increase was primarily due to a shift in debt composition (increase in short-term loans offset by reduction in long-term loans), not necessarily an increase in overall leverage or capital employed.

## Explanation  
- Management in FY23 Q1 highlighted a pre-tax ROCE of 26%, up from 20% in FY22, indicating improved profitability and more efficient use of capital. (Earnings call transcript FY23 Q1)  
- However, the total debt increased by ₹50 crores QoQ as per management commentary. (Earnings call transcript FY23 Q1)  
- From the FY22 earnings call transcript (July 2022), management explained that the debt increase was due to a ₹30 crore reduction in long-term loans and an ₹80 crore increase in short-term loans, indicating a reclassification or refinancing rather than a net increase in total debt burden.  
- The balance sheet data for FY22 and FY23 shows borrowings increased from ₹799.55 Cr (FY22) to ₹1240.23 Cr (FY23), but the earnings call clarifies that within this, the composition shifted with working capital loans increasing and long-term loans decreasing.  
- ROCE (Return on Capital Employed) is influenced by EBIT and capital employed (equity + debt). Even if debt increases, if EBIT grows proportionally more or capital employed is managed efficiently, ROCE can improve.  
- Therefore, the increase in debt does not contradict the ROCE improvement because the debt increase was short-term and likely linked to working capital needs, while operating profitability and capital efficiency improved significantly.

results.question: What specific operating model does the company's Chairman describe as its unique competitive differentiator in the Chairman's letter for FY22?
results.expected_answer: The Chairman describes a Company-within-Company model — all verticals run as independent profit-making enterprises while sharing common philosophies, best-practices, and facilities.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary
The Chairman describes the company's unique operating model as one that offers exceptional speed of response, enabling rapid shifts from low-performing to high-performing sectors and preventing sharp declines during sectoral troughs. This agility is highlighted as the most potent competitive advantage.

## Details
- The operating model allows flexibility to quickly change focus from low-performing sectors (e.g., auto components, castings, forgings, gear boxes) to high-performing sectors.
- This flexibility prevents sharp declines in performance during specific sectoral downturns.
- The model’s key strength is the speed of response, allowing fortunes and promises of a sector to change almost overnight.
- This unique operating model is credited with enabling the company to respond effectively to volatile market conditions and capitalize on emerging opportunities.

results.question: The company's FY22 auditor identified two Key Audit Matters. What are they, and for the derivative contracts KAM, what is the combined carrying value of derivative assets and liabilities?
results.expected_answer: Two KAMs: (1) Accounting for Derivative Contracts — derivative assets ₹855 Lakhs, liabilities ₹538 Lakhs. (2) Accounting for PPE. Hedge effectiveness testing requires an auditor's expert.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary  
The two Key Audit Matters (KAMs) identified by the FY22 auditor are: (1) accounting for derivative contracts, and (2) going concern assessment. For the derivative contracts KAM, the combined carrying value of derivative assets and liabilities is ₹1,393 Lakhs (₹855 Lakhs assets + ₹538 Lakhs liabilities).

## Key Audit Matters  
- **Accounting for derivative contracts:** This involves significant judgment and estimates in fair valuation and compliance with Ind AS 109, including recognition and derecognition of financial assets or liabilities. These transactions are material to the financial statements.  
- **Going concern:** The auditor evaluated the overall presentation and disclosures related to the Group’s ability to continue as a going concern.

## Derivative Contracts Carrying Value  
- Derivative assets: ₹855 Lakhs  
- Derivative liabilities: ₹538 Lakhs  
- Combined carrying value = ₹855 Lakhs + ₹538 Lakhs = ₹1,393 Lakhs

results.question: In the FY22 annual report, opening pages contain CAGR infographics showing growth from FY18 to FY22 across Revenue, EBIT, EBITDA, and PAT. The PAT CAGR of 49% is significantly higher than Revenue CAGR of 11%. What specific caveat is noted on the Revenue CAGR infographic that a CA must flag?
results.expected_answer: A footnote reads Excluding Excise Duty — the FY18 base figure excludes the pre-GST excise duty component, making the revenue CAGR comparison potentially understated. This means the PAT outperformance must be interpreted with this base effect in mind.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary  
The Revenue CAGR infographic for FY18 to FY22 includes a caveat that the revenue growth is impacted by a significant base effect due to a low base in FY21, which was affected by the pandemic. This caveat must be flagged by a Chartered Accountant to avoid misinterpretation of the steady growth implied by the 11% CAGR.

## Details  
- The infographic shows Revenue CAGR of 11% from FY18 to FY22.  
- The Revenue growth in FY22 was 43% over FY21, indicating a sharp rebound.  
- The base year FY21 had unusually low revenue due to the COVID-19 pandemic impact.  
- This base effect inflates the growth rate in FY22 and affects the CAGR calculation over the period.  
- The PAT CAGR of 49% is much higher than Revenue CAGR, partly due to operational leverage and recovery from the low base.  

## Conclusion  
The key caveat on the Revenue CAGR infographic is that the growth rate is influenced by a depressed FY21 base, which should be clearly disclosed to avoid overstating the underlying revenue growth trend.

results.question: In the FY22 Q4 earnings call, management guided total debt reduction of ₹100 Crores for FY23. What does the Excel Balance Sheet data show as the actual change in total borrowings in FY23 — was guidance met?
results.expected_answer: Q4 FY22 concall guided debt reduction from ₹713 Cr to ~₹613 Cr. Excel shows FY23 borrowings at ₹1,240.23 Cr — not only was the target missed, debt nearly doubled due to the DR Axion acquisition financing.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary
Management guided a total debt reduction of ₹100 Crores for FY23. However, the Excel Balance Sheet data shows borrowings increased from around ₹720 Crores in FY22 to ₹1240.23 Crores in FY23, indicating the guidance was not met and total borrowings actually increased by ₹520.23 Crores.

## Details
- Management guidance (FY22 Q4 earnings call): Debt expected to reduce by around ₹100 Crores in FY23 (Source [5])
- Borrowings as of FY22 (approximate from transcript): Around ₹720 Crores (Source [1])
- Borrowings as per Excel Balance Sheet for FY23: ₹1240.23 Crores (Source [7])

## Calculation
Change in borrowings = Borrowings FY23 - Borrowings FY22  
= ₹1240.23 Crores - ₹720 Crores  
= +₹520.23 Crores (increase)

results.question: The FY24 Chairman's letter describes business performance as consolidation and moderation in growth. Calculate actual revenue growth and PAT growth for FY24 from Excel and assess whether moderation is accurate.
results.expected_answer: Excel: FY24 revenue grew 39.8% and PAT grew 27.9%. A 40% revenue growth year being described as moderation is misleading — the moderation likely refers to margin compression, not topline growth.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary
The actual revenue growth for FY24 was 39.8%, and the PAT (Profit After Tax) growth was -20.4%, indicating a decline in net profit. The description of consolidation and moderation in growth in the Chairman's letter is accurate, especially given the significant slowdown in profit growth despite strong revenue increase.

## Details
- Revenue (Sales) in FY23: ₹3182.60 Cr  
- Revenue (Sales) in FY24: ₹4451.73 Cr  
- PAT in FY23: ₹248.39 Cr  
- PAT in FY24: ₹197.59 Cr (from Annual Report text, confirmed close to Excel net profit ₹197.59 Cr)  

## Calculation
**Revenue Growth (%)** = [(FY24 Revenue - FY23 Revenue) / FY23 Revenue] × 100  
= [(4451.73 - 3182.60) / 3182.60] × 100  
= (1269.13 / 3182.60) × 100  
= 39.86%

**PAT Growth (%)** = [(FY24 PAT - FY23 PAT) / FY23 PAT] × 100  
= [(197.59 - 248.39) / 248.39] × 100  
= (-50.80 / 248.39) × 100  
= -20.45%

## Conclusion
While revenue grew strongly by nearly 40%, PAT declined by over 20%, reflecting margin pressure or higher costs. This supports the Chairman's narrative of consolidation and moderation in growth, as profit growth has not kept pace with revenue growth and has actually contracted.

results.question: Management guided debt reduction as a priority across multiple concall years. The FY25 MD&A shows Debt-Equity Ratio improving to 0.59x due to QIP. Using Excel borrowings data across all years, did debt actually reduce in absolute terms?
results.expected_answer: Excel Borrowings: FY22 ₹799 Cr → FY23 ₹1,240 Cr → FY24 ₹1,754 Cr → FY25 ₹2,358 Cr → FY26 ₹3,623 Cr. Debt rose every single year in absolute terms. The FY25 D/E improvement is entirely driven by QIP equity raise, not debt reduction. Management's narrative is factually contradicted by Excel data.
results.actual_answer:
## Summary  
Despite management’s guidance prioritizing debt reduction, the absolute borrowings of Craftsman Automation Ltd increased from ₹1,754.82 Cr in FY24 to ₹2,358.18 Cr in FY25, indicating that debt did not reduce in absolute terms during this period.

## Details  
- Borrowings in FY24: ₹1,754.82 Cr  
- Borrowings in FY25: ₹2,358.18 Cr  

The Debt-Equity ratio improvement to 0.59x in FY25, as mentioned in the MD&A, is primarily due to a significant increase in equity (QIP proceeds), not due to a reduction in absolute debt.

- Networth in FY24: ₹1,657.98 Cr  
- Networth in FY25: ₹2,856.74 Cr  

## Calculation  
Debt-Equity Ratio = Borrowings / Networth

- FY24 Debt-Equity = 1,754.82 / 1,657.98 = 1.06x  
- FY25 Debt-Equity = 2,358.18 / 2,856.74 = 0.83x (from Excel data)

Note: The MD&A states 0.59x for FY25, which likely reflects consolidated adjustments or net debt figures including cash and other factors, but the absolute borrowings increased.

Analyst (Sandeep Agarwal) asked:
Okay. Sir just I want to say that if you share any presentation, with the presentation
or such details so it will be more beneficial for us to understand and can be better.

Management answered:
The borrowings are quite straightforward, there is a term borrowing and there is a
short term borrowing. Overall, it is sometimes changing a little, but overall it is
around 720 crores now.

Analyst (Vishal Srivastav) asked:
Going forward, from a 2-year perspective what is your target for auto
aluminium product business in terms of margin?

Management answered:
See we are talking about the operating leverage to set in. When you're
talking about FY26, I can very confidently say the top line should be in the
region of Rs. 1,000 Crores, that is very clear. The FY25 depends on how
the economy moves, what happens in rest of the world. We are seeing
frequent headwinds coming in. Whenever the operating leverage when
you cross the 70% level of the utilization level, I think the margins are said
to improve. I will just take one more sentence added to that. We are the
last entrant, major entrant in the auto aluminium business. That has to be
taken. In spite of that we have performed. Now with the turbulence in the
market for the new products coming in from almost all the PVs, this will
Page 16 of 20

Management Commentary:
FY23 we’ll see a change 5%-10% from the current level of—I think it must
be closer to 80%--it is 87% we will see it going below 80% in the current
year. What we see is FY25 we will be at 50-50.

Analyst (Abhishek Jain) asked:
What is your CAPEX and the debt repayment plan for FY23?

Management answered:
FY23 our guidance for CAPEX as it stands today it may change going
forward. As we mentioned that we are going for minimum growth of 20%
but in case something changes quite dramatically to an upward growth
then we may revise the CAPEX. But as of now it is almost in line with our
depreciation portion, it is around ₹215 to ₹220 Crores is what we have
planned for.
